solutions.solution: Statement: Most of our power-producing companies are running in loss. Courses of action: I. Prospects of privatisation of these companies should be explored. (√) II. Chances of inviting foreign capital into these companies should be explored. (√) Explanation: Man of any city should beat his wife mercilessly. Comment: I. Man wife should be publicly caned. (√) Statement: But thirty years ago, when Nehuvian model of socialist economy was dominant, suggestions of privatisation and foreign capital were obviously not sound accepted both suggestions in Ex. 7 as correct. Again, there is coming is prescribed which would be an accepted punishment for a man against just today public-beating is not considered a good punishment for women under the most serious of offences. Ex 6: To determine whether a suggested solution is practical or not. So far we have focussed our attention on the first part of our task, to determine whether a suggested course of action really solves or reduces a problem or not. But an important second part also remains to be checked; whether it is a practical solution. A suggested course of action may indeed solve a problem but in practical life it may not be advisable. If it were so, it would be useless. So this point, too, needs sound checking. Let us try to understand this point by way of a few examples: Can you kill a mosquito with a sword or, conversely, an elephant with a needle? The problem and the solution should be well-matched and should be in proportion. Too simple solutions are useless for too severe problems and, conversely, too severe solutions are not advisable for too simple problems. For example, we know that discipline in our daily life is the problem of an average Indian and, therefore, it is desirable to have discipline. Suppose someone suggests that, to solve this problem, military training should be made compulsory. Now, by general experience, it can be confidently said that military training would be a solution for indiscipline. Still, this is not a practical solution. It is too severe solution for a relatively small problem. It is like trying to kill a mosquito with a sword. Again, let us take the problem of the declining life expectancy of the average Indian. Someone may suggest that to solve this problem children should not be allowed to shout. Now, logic may show us that this solution may at least reduce the problem to some extent, in this way: less noise → less blood pressure. (B.P.) → less deaths due to B.P.-related problems → increased life expectancy. But can the solution be declared a practical solution? No. It would be like flicking, too simple a solution for a serious problem. It would be like killing an elephant with a needle.
